chunk_summary: Guidelines for transitioning children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) to rehabilitation focus on safe feeding progression, electrolyte management, and antibiotic use to prevent complications like refeeding syndrome and infections.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for transitioning from F-75 to F-100 or catch-up diets in SAM rehabilitation.
Learn the protocols for electrolyte supplementation during the transition phase to prevent refeeding syndrome.
Recognize the role of antibiotics in managing infections during SAM rehabilitation.
Identify discharge criteria and follow-up strategies for children recovering from SAM.
key_facts: Transition to F-100 or catch-up diet only when appetite improves, problems resolve, and edema reduces.
Weight gain of ≥5 g/kg/day for at least 3 consecutive days is a practical trigger for transition.
Potassium supplementation: 3–4 mEq/kg/day for at least two weeks; magnesium replacement includes an initial IM dose followed by oral/enteral supplementation.
Oral amoxicillin (15 mg/kg every 8 hours for 5 days) is standard for uncomplicated SAM; parenteral antibiotics are used for complicated cases.
Discharge criteria include clinical stability, resolved edema, good appetite, and upward weight trajectory.
reference_quote: Transition to rehabilitation is the highest-risk phase in SAM if done too fast. Move from F-75 to F-100 or catch-up diet only when appetite has clearly improved, major problems are resolving, and edema is minimal or reducing. (Source: Operational Guidelines on Facility Based Management of Children with Severe Acute Malnutrition)
document_key_topics: Transition feeding in severe acute malnutrition
Discharge and follow-up criteria for SAM recovery
